title: Hormuz Closes Again as Iran-Lebanon Linkage Threatens Deal
subhead: Iran shut the Strait of Hormuz for the second time since the ceasefire MoU, tying its reopening to an Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon. JD Vance opened talks in Switzerland, but the delegation briefly walked out over Trump's threats. Oil spiked, then partially recovered. Meanwhile, India's Russian oil imports hit a record, Libya posted its highest output since 2013, and Europe's central bankers warned inflation is far from beaten.

sections.heading: Hormuz Closed Again — This Time Over Lebanon
sections.body:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps announced on Saturday that the Strait of Hormuz would be closed again, citing Israeli military strikes in Lebanon as evidence the US-Iran ceasefire had been breached. According to shipping analytics firm Kpler, only five vessels transited the strait on Sunday, down from 26 the day before. The closure followed just days after Iran had eased its effective blockade following the June 18 US-Iran memorandum of understanding.

Hezbollah stated that Iran will not sign the final deal unless Israel withdraws from Lebanon, linking the waterway's fate directly to a separate conflict. Iran's semi-official Fars news agency reported that a revised version of the agreement includes language stating Iran and Oman would retain the right to decide the 'future administration of maritime services in the strait.' The Trump administration has called for unconditional opening and opposes any tolling system.

Brent crude spiked to $82.30 in early Asian trading before paring to around $81.04. WTI gained 2.5% to $77.77. ADNOC and Kuwait Petroleum have issued tenders offering buyers the option of loading crude either inside or outside the strait — a hedging mechanism that signals no confidence in stable transit anytime soon.
sections.whyItMatters: The Lebanon linkage transforms Hormuz from a US-Iran bilateral issue into a regional one with far more veto players. Each closure-and-reopening cycle erodes shipper confidence and adds risk premiums that outlast the closures themselves.

sections.heading: Switzerland Talks Resume After Walkout, but Framework Is Fragile
sections.body:
US-Iran negotiations in Switzerland produced a 60-day roadmap for continued talks on sanctions, nuclear issues, and implementing the ceasefire MoU. Vice President JD Vance leads the American delegation alongside Jared Kushner and Special Envoy Steve Witkoff. Qatar and Pakistan mediated after the Iranian delegation briefly walked out over a Trump social media post threatening military action if Tehran did not rein in allied groups in Lebanon.

Iran's chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf told state media that Tehran would not be intimidated by public threats. The 14-point MoU includes reopening Hormuz with immediate effect, unfreezing Iranian assets, and granting waivers for Iran to increase crude exports — but the formal signing ceremony, originally planned for Geneva this week, is now contingent on the Lebanon issue being resolved.

Meanwhile, France, Britain, and the Netherlands are pushing plans for a multinational naval mission to safeguard Hormuz shipping. The Netherlands has redirected a frigate toward the strait, and Germany has deployed two ships to the Red Sea in preparation for a possible Hormuz mission. Whether Iran would accept a Western-led naval presence in the strait remains an open question.
sections.whyItMatters: The 60-day roadmap buys time but the walkout shows how fragile the process is. Each public threat from Trump can scupper days of quiet diplomacy, and multinational naval planning moves forward whether Iran consents or not.

sections.heading: India's Russian Oil Imports Hit Record as Refiners Front-Load Ahead of Hormuz Normalisation
sections.body:
India imported an average of 2.66 million bpd from Russia in June through June 19, up from 1.91 million bpd in May, according to Kpler data — a record that cements Moscow as India's top supplier by a wide margin. UAE shipments stood at 636,000 bpd, near May's record of 644,000 bpd. Venezuela emerged as India's fourth-largest supplier at 209,000 bpd, while US imports dropped sharply to 91,000 bpd from 252,000 bpd in May.

Three Indian-flagged oil tankers carrying over 860,000 tonnes of crude and an Indian LNG carrier have resumed transiting Hormuz following the ceasefire agreement. Kpler analyst Sumit Ritolia expects recovery to be sequential: LPG first, then LNG, then crude — with full normalisation taking weeks or months as shipping companies, insurers, and traders rebuild confidence in the route.

India's strategy has been to diversify away from Gulf dependence. Russian crude continues to anchor the import basket due to discounts and supply security. Pre-war, the Gulf supplied roughly half of India's crude imports, two-thirds of its LNG, and nearly 90% of its LPG. Those proportions are unlikely to return to pre-crisis levels even after Hormuz normalises.
sections.whyItMatters: India's diversification is the clearest evidence that the Hormuz shock has permanently reshaped trade flows. Even with the strait reopening, the country's import mix has shifted decisively toward Russia — a change that outlasts any single diplomatic breakthrough.

sections.heading: Libya Hits Highest Oil Output Since 2013 as IEA Flags 2027 Surplus
sections.body:
Libya's National Oil Corporation reported total daily production of 1,487,723 bpd on June 21 — with crude at 1,438,560 bpd and condensate at 49,163 bpd — marking the highest output since 2013. NOC chairman Masoud Suleiman praised local companies and set a target of reaching 1.5 million bpd before year-end.

The Libyan milestone comes as the IEA's June 17 monthly oil market report projected a significant supply surplus in 2027 once Hormuz flows normalise and Iranian exports return under the deal's waivers. ICICI Bank Research echoes this view, forecasting a net supply deficit of 1.6 million bpd through 2026 but a 2 million bpd surplus in 2027, driving Brent down to a $65-75 range — contingent on no further geopolitical disruptions and global growth holding near 3%.

For now, ICICI sees Brent trading in a $75-85 band through H2 2026, with GCC production reaching 82% of pre-conflict capacity by September and 90% by December. US strategic reserves have drawn down 30 million barrels since February, and global inventory withdrawals averaged nearly 16 million bpd between March and May.
sections.whyItMatters: Libya's output surge and the IEA's surplus forecast point to the same reality: the market is pricing a supply wall in 2027. But between now and then, inventories are thin, GCC ramp-up is gradual, and any Hormuz disruption sends prices right back up.

sections.heading: Europe's Central Banks Keep Hawkish Stance Despite Iran Deal
sections.body:
ECB chief economist Philip Lane said the bank will remain 'proactive' in its fight against high inflation even after the US-Iran deal brought down energy prices, according to Reuters. ECB Governing Council member Gabriel Makhlouf separately warned that damage to energy infrastructure could cause lingering price pressures even with the ceasefire in place — a nod to the fact that reopened shipping lanes don't immediately restore damaged or rerouted supply chains.

ECB policymaker Pierre Wunsch told Reuters that the ECB may raise rates one more time as soon as July if inflation spreads beyond energy into core categories. The Bank of Japan raised rates to a 31-year high earlier in June, explicitly citing the Iran-war-induced energy shock as an inflation driver and flagging further hikes.

The pattern is consistent: energy shocks propagate into core inflation with a lag, and central bankers are reluctant to declare victory while the Hormuz situation remains volatile. Iran's latest closure — and the commodity price spike that followed — reinforces their caution.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran deal brought oil off its highs, but it hasn't brought inflation down to target. Central banks are treating the deal as a first step, not a resolution — and Hormuz's repeated closures give them cover to stay hawkish longer.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Alliance and Shadow Fleet Crackdown Intensify
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in France, leaders established a critical minerals alliance and crisis platform designed to cut reliance on China for rare earths, lithium, cobalt, and graphite. The initiative includes stockpiling alignment and an expanded role for the IAEA in supply chain monitoring. Canada's Prime Minister Mark Carney said the Iran deal 'exceeded expectations' and simultaneously announced new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet, energy revenues, defence-industrial sector, and disinformation networks.

The UK separately imposed sanctions on Yandex Bank, two other Russian lenders, and dozens of shadow fleet vessels exporting Russian oil. An Indian national — the captain of a shadow fleet tanker — was arrested in the UK, marking one of the first individual prosecutions linked to Russia's sanctions-evading tanker network.

Ukraine continued its energy strikes on Russia, with drones hitting Moscow's Kapotnya refinery for the second time in a week on June 19. Ukraine also struck targets in occupied Crimea, maintaining pressure on Russian energy infrastructure even as the G7 discussed tightening the sanctions net around Moscow's export channels.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 minerals alliance is a supply-chain decoupling move with real consequences for battery and semiconductor manufacturing. The shadow fleet sanctions and individual arrests signal a shift from naming ships to prosecuting people — raising the cost of evasion for owners, insurers, and crews alike.

bottomLine: Iran's decision to close Hormuz again — tying it to Israel's Lebanon campaign — turns a bilateral US-Iran dispute into a multi-front regional negotiation with no clear resolution path. The 60-day Swiss roadmap is a framework, not a solution, and every day of closure adds risk premium that central banks count as inflation. India's record Russian imports, Libya's decade-high output, and the IEA's 2027 surplus forecast all point to the same dynamic: the market expects supply to flood back next year, but right now inventories are thin and Hormuz keeps closing. The G7 minerals alliance and shadow fleet arrests are moves on a longer board — they matter more for 2027 than for tomorrow's Brent price.
sources: Reuters, Firstpost, Yahoo Finance/Benzinga, Libya Herald, Xinhua, Kpler via Rediff, ICICI Bank Research via ANI, ECB via Reuters, Africa Policy Institute via The Star (Kenya), Reuters (G7 critical minerals), Reuters (UK/Canada sanctions), Reuters (Ukraine drone strikes), Reuters (France/Britain/Netherlands Hormuz mission)
